pageDescription: Discover what steps I took to begin writing my PHP engine.
content: [{"id":"30f4e035-a64f-42b9-94cf-c94772249883","component":"Text","data":{"content":"My first question was simple: which PHP features do I actually want to support? String interpolation? Variable variables? Dynamic class instantiation? The kind of stuff that makes you either a genius or completely lose your mind.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"12637d74-57ff-48bb-b911-d76f3f4ac492","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The work ahead quickly became clear. I needed a lexer to turn source code into a token stream, a parser to turn that into an abstract syntax tree, and finally a way to actually execute it. On top of that, I had to handle syntax errors without making the whole thing explode.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"e8bc388c-b17a-46d9-8430-36799dc47d42","component":"Text","data":{"content":"At this point, I had seen the visitor pattern before but had never really understood its purpose or why it was used. Later in the project I came to realise: I've been a absolute sausage ignoring it.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"767b7df1-08d0-4438-9154-4249f106a96a","component":"Divider","data":{"variant":"line","size":"md","label":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"726d6860-29c7-4a6b-8d71-862a28aa3177","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Code Lexer","tag":"h2","align":"left","weight":"bold","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"20px","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"c29d4fce-bbc0-4ebc-ab6b-8fedc92e8ed0","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Beginning with the first challenge: code lexing and tokenizing. The file would be loaded as a string, and I had been planning how to push myself to build a tokenizer that used as little memory as possible. Using Span<char> allowed me to avoid allocations almost everywhere. Since a Span cannot be captured off the stack, I could take advantage of the stack’s fast 1MB memory block. By loading the source into a string, calling .AsSpan(), and iterating over it one character at a time, I could build the token stream efficiently and with minimal overhead.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"19a0c1d1-6f46-4020-948d-cb49dd88fd70","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Next, I moved on to designing the token data structure. Should I use a record, a class, or a struct? Classes always live on the heap, structs usually live on the stack, and while there are some edge cases where structs end up on the heap, I was careful to avoid those. Records can add extra overhead in certain scenarios, especially with copying and equality checks. In the end, I decided to go with a contiguous struct. It is fast, predictable, and memory-friendly, just the way I like it.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"4df35fd8-84d7-4269-8fcb-c86b64f1d4ff","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The first fields I decided to capture were the range start and range end. Instead of doing string comparisons and creating unnecessary string allocations, I already had access to the source via a Span<char>. I could grab the sequence only when I actually needed it. For most tokens, apart from literals, identifiers, and variables, I didn’t even need the token value. This also helped avoid unnecessary GC pressure from reference types lingering around like unwanted party guests.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"4a3978eb-9b8a-4cce-a126-9733082b333a","component":"Text","data":{"content":"RangeStart and RangeEnd are signed Int32s, which means four bytes each. That puts the struct at eight bytes so far. I still needed a way to mark the token type for the AST parser. I went with a byte-backed enum since there would never be more than 255 token kinds. That brings the struct’s total size to nine bytes, though CPUs love alignment, so it would likely be padded to 12 bytes. Efficient, predictable, and ready to churn through source code without complaint. The enum route also allows for nice pattern matching which JIT can inline incredibly well, when the JIT starts moving the tokeniser through JIT tiers, parsing happens in sub ms time. #BigUpRyuJIT","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"a6ba6496-7028-4053-8edc-61a9cc98e40e","component":"Divider","data":{"variant":"line","size":"md","label":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"10b7b547-a745-41f6-afcd-46bb76fc60d4","component":"Text","data":{"content":"AST Parser/ Syntax Tree","tag":"h2","align":"left","weight":"bold","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"20px","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"47583a1b-a094-4d17-9e5a-c808e4f58cfc","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The next thing I had to plan, once the tokenizer was complete, was parsing the AST. My friend and ex-colleague recommended using productions, which let you define grammar rules and then combine them to match more complex sequences. I wanted to challenge myself here as well. My plan was to use Span<Token> with in, just like I did with the source span. Using in signals that the span itself won’t be reassigned in the method, which avoids unnecessary copies and gives a small performance boost while keeping the parser efficient.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"f768c461-5008-4178-a43f-1b371b0dae0c","component":"Text","data":{"content":"To use combinators effectively, I needed to track the current pointer in the token array. Every grammar rule had to generate a valid AST node and advance the pointer. It also needed to check for the longest match first, handle operator precedence according to PHP’s guide, and be able to roll back when necessary.  To manage all of this efficiently, I decided to create a ref struct. Using a ref struct has a few big advantages. First, it cannot escape to the heap, so all of its state stays on the stack, which avoids unnecessary allocations and reduces GC pressure. Second, it allows me to pass both the source span and the token span by reference without copying the spans themselves. Every time a token matched or the parser rewound, the struct could be updated in place, keeping parsing lightning fast and memory efficient.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"21f4d7d6-21b8-48d7-a7d1-f87abd6c3bd1","component":"Text","data":{"content":"To keep the combinators as modular as possible, I used a static Parser class. Inside it, a switch on the current token kind allowed me to skip iterating through every grammar rule, creating a hot path that the JIT could aggressively optimize. This made recursive descent parsing straightforward and naturally led to a Pratt parser for handling operator precedence efficiently. Once the JIT warmed up and started moving code through its tiers, parsing happened in sub-millisecond time, even for large token streams. Again, #BigUpRyuJIT.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"320fcfad-cc46-4113-8698-ae5a948f51a9","component":"Divider","data":{"variant":"line","size":"md","label":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"6337bc1e-d9a5-48a1-80c2-280744c685b7","component":"Text","data":{"content":"IL Production/ Node Visitors.","tag":"h2","align":"left","weight":"bold","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"20px","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"5100f040-dcc8-457c-beca-21dd71cadefd","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This is where it got really exciting. At this point, I wasn’t sure how I was going to take the AST and actually execute it. I knew I needed to generate IL, but the path forward was unclear. My first thought was to recursively iterate over the AST’s children and handle execution that way. I tried it, but no matter how I structured it, I didn’t like how it ran or how it “felt” in code.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"53a852fe-3bd1-4fa7-b085-ae6aae6f0c01","component":"Text","data":{"content":"I spoke with my friend and ex-colleague, and he told me about the visitor pattern and how it could simplify execution. I added it a little reservedly at first, but as I dug deeper, I realized how much visitors could do: type detection, dead code removal, and more. That’s when I got hooked and knew this was the right approach.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"5f94bc18-3b34-41b1-815a-6959430ef561","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The final step was adding the ILGeneration visitor. I knew IL could be brutal, and InvalidProgramException(\"The CLR detected an Invalid Program\") had haunted me more times than I could count. To make the process manageable, I created an ILGenerator wrapper. At first, I considered extending ILGenerator, but it is sealed, so that was not possible. Instead, I took a facade approach: I built a class with the same call signatures as the original ILGenerator that also logged every instruction as it was emitted.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"d47a2c33-05b9-4573-9c37-8999b9948125","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This wrapper allowed me to track the evaluation stack at every step, see exactly which opcodes were being pushed, popped, or boxed, and understand how branches, locals, and method calls were interacting. By capturing the sequence of emitted instructions, I could detect mismatched stack states, incorrect opcode order, and other subtle IL errors that normally only manifest as runtime exceptions. This approach transformed IL generation from an opaque, error-prone process into a precise, debuggable workflow, making it far easier to reason about the generated code and fix mistakes efficiently. It was here that visitors finally clicked, it was like this black box just exploded and revealed its contents, I could visualise the entire process from parsing to execution.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]
